baseline_dominance.justification: The only surviving differentiator is the adversarial-AS predicate proof (rfc7662_advocate Attack 3 concedes 'no AS-side filter can produce a predicate proof over inputs the AS does not observe at filter time'). However, the construction does not lead with this as a formal theorem. RAR + JWT introspection (auth0_pm Attack 1), SD-JWT (spiffe_engineer Attack 1), and WIMSE sender-constrained tokens all approximate selective disclosure for the non-adversarial-AS case. The adversarial-AS property is real but not yet formally stated as the distinguishing claim — baseline can approximate the other claimed properties.

formal_security_argument.justification: Threat model exists (adversarial AS sees all credentialCommitments), named assumption (Poseidon PRF, A2), and SSZK game sketch with the nullifierSecret fix. However: (1) public input set is not enumerated — cryptographer Attack 1 shows credentialCommitment linkability is unproven without specifying which inputs are public vs private; (2) HVZK vs MVZK distinction not addressed — cryptographer Attack 2 shows a malicious RS choosing structured nonces can reduce ZK guarantees; (3) nonce generation oracle unspecified; (4) trusted setup trust model for AgentPolicy/Delegation Groth16 keys not documented (cryptographer Attack 3). Reduction sketch is present but incomplete on multiple axes.

implementability.justification: Construction maps directly to existing Bolyra primitives: Poseidon3 hash (already in circuit stack), Groth16 proving (existing AgentPolicy circuit), BabyJubjub EdDSA (existing credential signing). Cost is explicitly stated: ~25 extra constraints for nullifierSecret addition (0.3% increase over existing circuit). This is well within Phase 1 budget. The nullifier scheme reuses the existing sessionNonce binding pattern.

adversarial_survival.justification: Tier 3 surfaced multiple attacks the construction cannot answer: (1) cryptographer — credentialCommitment linkability across RS sessions unaddressed, HVZK insufficient against malicious RS, trusted setup trust model undocumented; (2) cu_ciso — NCUA Part 748 audit trail void (boolean proof receipt insufficient), Poseidon has no FIPS 140-3 certification (existential gap for CU market), nullifierSecret has no key management SOP; (3) spiffe_engineer — WIMSE sender-constrained tokens already provide AS-blind presentation, two-identity-planes operational cost unaddressed; (4) auth0_pm — nullifierSecret key lifecycle unspecified, revocation latency gap vs RFC 7662. The majority of persona attacks are marked 'No — construction must address' and remain unanswered.

scenario_fit.justification: Two scenarios named but neither is load-bearing with a named stakeholder: (1) '2^64 permission space' is explicitly demolished by auth0_pm Attack 3 as non-market — no production system uses 2^64 permissions, and Bolyra's own model is 8-bit; (2) 'semi-trusted AS' is the real scenario but lacks a named credit union, regulatory requirement reference, or concrete deployment. The cu_ciso attacks show the CU deployment path has existential gaps (FIPS, audit trails, revocation SLA). No named stakeholder or regulatory mandate is tied to the surviving adversarial-AS property.

gaps: Lead with adversarial-AS predicate proof as a formal theorem — state it as: 'no AS-side filter can produce a predicate proof over inputs the AS does not observe at filter time' and prove baseline cannot match
Enumerate the full public input set of the circuit (which values does the RS see?) and prove RS-side unlinkability via an anonymity game
Specify nonce generation: who generates sessionNonce, how its distribution is constrained, and prove security against malicious-verifier (MVZK) RS
Document the AgentPolicy/Delegation Groth16 trusted setup ceremony (participants, threshold, transparency log) or restrict claims to PLONK path
Add nullifierSecret key lifecycle: generation method, storage requirements, rotation policy, compromise impact, and mapping to GLBA §314.4(c) key management
Add a FIPS compliance bridge: e.g., wrap the ZK proof output in a FIPS-approved signature (ECDSA/EdDSA) so the cryptographic module presented to examiners is NIST-approved
Define a proof-receipt audit schema for NCUA Part 748: {circuitId, merkleRoot, nullifierHash, predicateSatisfied, timestamp} with reconstruction guarantees
Address revocation latency: specify on-chain revocation SLA, operator runbook for Tier 1 ops, and comparison column vs RFC 7662 real-time revocation

Replace the 2^64 permission space scenario with a concrete CU deployment: e.g., AI treasury agent holding FINANCIAL_SMALL + READ_DATA where RS must not learn higher permissions exist, with named regulatory driver
Address WIMSE/SPIFFE integration: either specify a SPIRE workload attestor plugin bridge or argue why a separate identity plane is justified for the target deployer
